questions.question: A factory can produce 500 microchips or 100 motherboard units per day. The factory decides to produce 25 more motherboard units. What is the opportunity cost in microchips?
options: 25 microchips, matching the increase in motherboard production directly
125 microchips, reflecting the trade-off ratio of five microchips per motherboard
100 microchips, equal to the total daily motherboard production capacity
500 microchips, representing the entire microchip manufacturing capacity
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 motherboard = $500 \div 100 = 5$ microchips. For 25 additional motherboards: $25 \times 5 = 125$ microchips sacrificed.

questions.question: Country X produces 50 units of Wheat using 100 labor hours. Country Y produces 40 units of Wheat using 120 labor hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in Wheat?
options: Country Y, because it produces a larger total quantity of Wheat output
Country X, because it requires 2 hours per unit versus Country Y's 3 hours per unit
Country Y, because it uses more total labor hours in wheat production
Neither country, because both produce wheat using different total hours
questions.explanation: Country X requires $100/50 = 2$ hours per unit. Country Y requires $120/40 = 3$ hours per unit. Country X is more productive per hour.

questions.question: Country East can produce 800 units of Steel or 400 units of Aluminum. Country West can produce 300 units of Steel or 200 units of Aluminum. Which country has the comparative advantage in Aluminum?
options: Country East, because it produces more Steel and Aluminum in absolute terms
Country West, because its opportunity cost of Aluminum is 1.5 Steel versus East's 2 Steel
Country East, because its Aluminum output per worker exceeds West's Aluminum output
Country West, because it can produce Steel and Aluminum in nearly equal amounts
questions.explanation: East's cost of 1 Aluminum = $800/400 = 2$ Steel. West's cost = $300/200 = 1.5$ Steel. West has the lower relative cost in Aluminum.

questions.question: An economy on its PPF produces 100 units of Good A and 50 units of Good B. It wants to produce 110 units of A and 60 units of B. What must occur for this to be possible?
options: A reallocation of resources from Good B production toward Good A production
The point is unattainable without economic growth or a technological breakthrough
An increase in the price of Good A to make its production more profitable
A reduction in unemployment to bring idle resources into productive use
questions.explanation: An economy on its PPF is using all resources fully and efficiently. Increasing both outputs simultaneously is impossible without expanding productive capacity.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, unit labor requirements are $a_{LC} = 3$ and $a_{LW} = 9$. What is the autarky relative price of Wine ($P_W/P_C$)?
options: 0.33, calculated as the inverse of the labor requirement ratio between goods
3, equal to the ratio of the unit labor requirements $a_{LW}/a_{LC}$
6, the arithmetic average of the two unit labor requirements
27, the product of both unit labor requirements multiplied together
questions.explanation: Autarky relative price $P_W/P_C = a_{LW}/a_{LC} = 9/3 = 3$. One unit of Wine costs 3 units of Cloth.

questions.question: On the World Relative Supply curve, what does a flat horizontal segment indicate about the trading countries?
options: Both countries have fully specialized in their respective comparative advantage goods
At least one country is not fully specialized and is indifferent between the two goods
Neither country finds trade to be economically beneficial at that relative price
Global production of both goods has reached its absolute maximum possible capacity
questions.explanation: When the world price equals a country's internal opportunity cost, that country earns no extra gain from specializing and can produce any mix along its PPF.

questions.question: World Relative Demand intersects World Relative Supply on a vertical section. What does this imply about the distribution of gains from trade?
options: Only one country captures all the gains while the other gains nothing from trade
Both countries generally share in the gains because the price is between their opportunity costs
Neither country gains from trade because the equilibrium is on a vertical supply section
The distribution of gains cannot be determined from the shape of the supply curve
questions.explanation: A vertical RS section occurs between the two countries' opportunity costs. Both countries benefit because the world price is better than each one's autarky price.

questions.question: Home productivity is 10 times higher than Foreign's in every industry, but Home wages are only 5 times higher. What is the trade outcome?
options: Foreign will export all goods because its absolute wages are much lower than Home's
Home will export all goods because its productivity advantage exceeds its wage premium
Trade will be balanced with each country exporting exactly half of all goods produced
No trade will occur because absolute advantage determines all trade patterns
questions.explanation: Home's productivity advantage ($10\times$) exceeds its wage premium ($5\times$), giving Home lower unit labor costs in every sector. Home exports all goods.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, how does trade affect the real wage of workers measured in terms of the good their country imports?
options: The real wage in terms of the imported good decreases because trade raises its price
The real wage in terms of the imported good increases because trade lowers its relative price
The real wage remains unchanged because it is determined solely by domestic productivity
The real wage fluctuates unpredictably depending on exchange rate movements between partners
questions.explanation: Trade reduces the relative price of imported goods. Workers can purchase more imports with their nominal earnings from the export sector.

questions.question: In a many-good Ricardian model, the relative wage ($w/w^*$) increases. What happens to the number of goods produced at Home?
options: The number of goods increases because higher wages attract more workers
The number of goods decreases as Home becomes less competitive in marginal industries
The number of goods remains unchanged because productivity determines production
The number of goods increases for luxury items but decreases for basic necessities
questions.explanation: A higher relative wage shifts the cutoff point. More goods become cheaper to import than to produce domestically, reducing the range of Home production.

questions.question: Under free trade in the Ricardian model, what determines the slope of the Consumption Possibility Frontier?
options: The domestic autarky price ratio determined by unit labor requirements
The world relative price ratio established through international exchange
The ratio of domestic to foreign wages in the two trading countries
The marginal rate of substitution between the two goods for all consumers
questions.explanation: Under free trade, consumers face international prices. The CPF slope equals the world relative price, not the domestic opportunity cost.

questions.question: A policy makes the wealthy 20% richer and the poor 5% poorer, but total aggregate income increases. How is this outcome classified in welfare economics?
options: An actual Pareto Improvement because total income has increased overall
A potential Pareto Improvement, since the gains could theoretically compensate the losses
Neither an improvement nor a deterioration because the effects cancel each other out
A Pareto deterioration because at least one group has been made strictly worse off
questions.explanation: It is not an actual Pareto Improvement because some are worse off. But total gains exceed losses, making compensation theoretically possible (Kaldor-Hicks).

questions.question: Which condition must hold for an allocation of resources to be considered Pareto Efficient in production?
options: All firms in every industry must earn exactly zero economic profit in equilibrium
The Marginal Rate of Technical Substitution must be identical across all producing industries
Consumer utility must be maximized subject to each individual's personal budget constraint
The price of every good must equal its average cost of production in every single firm
questions.explanation: When MRTS is equal across all industries, resources cannot be reallocated to increase one output without reducing another, achieving production efficiency.

questions.question: $P_X = 120$, $a_{LX} = 4$, $P_Y = 150$, and $a_{LY} = 6$ in the Ricardian model. Which sector offers the higher nominal wage?
options: Sector Y, because the market price per unit of Good Y is higher than Good X
Sector X, where the wage is 30 compared to Sector Y's wage of only 25
Sector Y, because its lower unit labor requirement signals higher productivity
Both sectors offer exactly the same nominal wage of 30 per hour to workers
questions.explanation: Wage in X = $120/4 = 30$. Wage in Y = $150/6 = 25$. Workers migrate to the higher-paying Sector X.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, labor is assumed to be perfectly mobile between domestic sectors. What is the assumption about international labor mobility?
options: Labor is also perfectly mobile between countries, seeking the highest global wage
Labor is completely immobile between countries, preserving international wage differences
Labor mobility between countries is determined by government immigration policies
The model makes no assumption about international mobility because it is irrelevant
questions.explanation: The model assumes factors cannot move across borders. This international immobility is the fundamental reason national wages and prices differ.

questions.question: Unit labor requirements are $a_{LA} = 2$ and $a_{LB} = 5$. The world relative price $P_A/P_B$ is 0.5. Which good will this country produce and export?
options: Only Good B, because its internal cost of B is lower than the world price
Only Good A, because the world price of A exceeds its domestic opportunity cost
Both goods equally, splitting the available labor force between the two sectors
Neither good, because the world price equals the domestic opportunity cost exactly
questions.explanation: Internal opportunity cost of A = $2/5 = 0.4$ units of B. World price of A ($0.5$ B) exceeds domestic cost. Country specializes in and exports Good A.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, a tariff raises the price of an import-competing good. What happens to the return on capital specific to that protected sector?
options: The return decreases because the tariff raises input costs for domestic firms
The return increases as higher sector revenue flows to the fixed capital owners
The return remains constant because capital returns are set in global markets
The return shifts to the export sector as capital is quickly redeployed there
questions.explanation: The tariff raises the domestic price, increasing total sector revenue. With capital fixed and immobile, the additional surplus accrues to capital owners.

questions.question: Why does the immobile factor model suggest that trade can potentially decrease aggregate welfare when adjustment costs are considered?
options: Trade always reduces total national income in every version of this model
Workers in declining industries face permanent losses and cannot transition to growing sectors
The government must always subsidize declining industries, creating large budget deficits
Immobile factors automatically depreciate in value the moment any trade is opened
questions.explanation: Gains from trade depend on resource reallocation. Without factor mobility, the social costs borne by trapped workers and capital can exceed the efficiency gains from trade.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the export price rises while the import price stays constant. How is a worker in the import-competing sector affected?
options: Real income rises because a booming export sector lifts wages for all workers
Real income falls because export goods become more expensive while their nominal wage is stuck
Real income remains constant because their own sector's prices have not changed at all
Nominal income rises in their sector but their real purchasing power is unchanged
questions.explanation: Nominal wages are stuck in the import sector where prices did not rise, but the export goods they consume have become more expensive, reducing real purchasing power.

questions.question: Country 1 has 500 capital and 500 labor. Country 2 has 200 capital and 300 labor. Which country is relatively capital-abundant?
options: Country 2, because it has fewer total units of capital than Country 1 has
Country 1, because its K/L ratio of 1.0 exceeds Country 2's ratio of 0.67
Country 2, because a smaller total economy is always more capital-abundant
Neither country, because both have at least as much labor as capital in absolute terms
questions.explanation: Country 1's K/L = $500/500 = 1.0$. Country 2's K/L = $200/300 \approx 0.67$. Country 1 has more capital per worker and is capital-abundant.

questions.question: According to the Heckscher-Ohlin model, international trade between two countries will eventually lead to the convergence of which economic variables?
options: Only the absolute productivity levels in each sector across the trading nations
Both goods prices and factor prices, including wages and rental rates of capital
The total size of the labor force and capital stock in each national economy
Government tariff rates and trade policy preferences among the voting population
questions.explanation: The Factor Price Equalization theorem states that trading goods effectively equalizes both goods prices and factor prices (wages and rents) across countries.

questions.question: What was the central finding of the Leontief Paradox regarding United States trade patterns?
options: The US exported more to developed countries than to developing countries overall
The US, despite being capital-abundant, appeared to export labor-intensive goods
US imports and exports were roughly equal in both capital and labor intensity
Factor prices between the US and its trading partners had completely equalized
questions.explanation: Leontief's 1953 study found US exports were less capital-intensive than US imports, contradicting the basic H-O prediction for a capital-rich country.

questions.question: The price of a capital-intensive good rises. According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, what happens to the real return to labor?
options: Real wages rise because higher goods prices benefit all factors of production equally
The real return to labor decreases as capital's return rises at the expense of labor
Real wages remain constant because they are determined solely by labor productivity
The real return to labor rises for skilled workers but falls for unskilled workers
questions.explanation: The factor used intensively in the price-rising sector (capital) gains in real terms, while the other factor (labor) experiences a decline in its real purchasing power.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, what is the effect of an increase in the total supply of mobile labor on the two sectors' output?
options: Output rises in the labor-intensive sector but falls in the capital-intensive sector
The output of both sectors increases as the now cheaper labor is hired by both industries
Output remains unchanged because specific factors limit total production capacity
Output falls in both sectors as wages are driven down by the increased labor supply
questions.explanation: More plentiful and cheaper labor leads both sectors to hire additional workers. With fixed specific factors, total output expands in each sector.

questions.question: $P_M$ rises by 20% while the nominal wage rises by only 10% in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the real wage measured in terms of Manufactured goods?
options: The real wage rises because the nominal wage increased by 10% overall
The real wage falls because the price of manufactured goods rose faster than the wage
The real wage remains constant because the two changes exactly offset each other
The real wage rises in nominal terms but falls in real purchasing power terms
questions.explanation: Since $P_M$ rose by $20\%$ while $W$ rose by only $10\%$, the purchasing power of the wage over manufactured goods has decreased. Real wage in terms of M falls.

questions.question: The stock of Land specific to Agriculture increases in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the nominal wage of labor?
options: The nominal wage decreases because more land reduces the need for farm workers
The nominal wage increases as higher land productivity raises labor's marginal product
The nominal wage remains constant because wages are set in global labor markets
The nominal wage falls in manufacturing but rises in the agricultural sector only
questions.explanation: More land raises the marginal productivity of labor working in agriculture. The labor demand curve shifts outward, bidding up the economy-wide nominal wage.

questions.question: How does the Specific Factor Model explain the political divide between different groups of capital owners regarding trade policy?
options: All capital owners everywhere oppose free trade because it lowers all profit rates
Export sector capital owners favor free trade while import-competing capital owners oppose it
Capital owners are always indifferent to trade because they can move their assets freely
The model predicts that capital owners never take political positions on trade issues
questions.explanation: Because capital is stuck in specific industries, its owners' welfare depends on their sector's output price. Export sector capital gains from trade; import sector capital loses.

questions.question: In the Krugman model, two countries have completely identical factor endowments and technologies. What enables them to gain from trading with each other?
options: One country must have an absolute advantage in producing at least one of the goods
Product differentiation combined with economies of scale allows specialization and larger scale
Identical countries cannot gain from trade under any theoretical framework in economics
Government subsidies are required to create a cost advantage that makes trade beneficial
questions.explanation: Trade allows each country to produce a narrower range of product varieties at larger scale, lowering average costs while consumers enjoy access to foreign varieties.

questions.question: An industry is characterized by internal economies of scale, where larger firms have lower average costs. What market structure is most likely to develop?
options: Perfect competition with many small price-taking firms producing identical products
Monopolistic competition or oligopoly, because large firms have cost advantages over small ones
A pure monopoly where only a single firm can survive in the entire global market
The market structure is determined by government regulation, not by cost structures
questions.explanation: Internal economies mean larger firms produce at lower cost. Small firms are disadvantaged, leading to market structures dominated by larger players with pricing power.

questions.question: What is agglomeration in the context of New Economic Geography and external economies of scale?
options: The merger of many small firms into a single large multinational corporate entity
The geographic clustering of firms in one location to capture shared external benefits
The process of moving production facilities to countries with the lowest wage costs
The division of a single production process into stages spread across many countries
questions.explanation: Agglomeration occurs when firms locate near each other to benefit from thick labor markets, specialized suppliers, and knowledge spillovers that lower costs for all.

questions.question: A large country can potentially increase its national welfare by imposing a tariff. Under what condition does this occur?
options: The tariff must be set high enough to completely eliminate all imports of the good
The terms-of-trade gain from lower world prices must exceed the domestic deadweight loss
Any positive tariff always increases welfare for a large country regardless of its rate
The tariff must be matched by equivalent export subsidies to balance the welfare effects
questions.explanation: By reducing import demand, the large country forces foreign exporters to accept lower prices. If that gain exceeds the efficiency loss, national welfare rises.

questions.question: What is the consumption distortion component of the deadweight loss caused by a tariff?
options: The government revenue that is collected from consumers who continue buying imports
The welfare loss from consumers who reduce or stop purchasing the good due to higher prices
The shift in consumer spending toward domestically produced substitute products
The increase in consumer surplus that results from protecting domestic industries
questions.explanation: Higher prices force some consumers out of the market entirely. The utility they would have gained from purchasing at world prices is lost forever and not captured by anyone.

questions.question: A small country provides an export subsidy to its domestic producers. What are the effects on the domestic price and national welfare?
options: Domestic price falls and national welfare increases due to greater global competitiveness
Domestic price rises and national welfare decreases due to distortions and fiscal costs
Domestic price remains at the world level and national welfare is completely unchanged
Domestic price falls for consumers but rises for producers, with zero net welfare effect
questions.explanation: Producers will only sell domestically if the price matches the export return (world price + subsidy). The resulting distortions plus the fiscal cost reduce national welfare.

questions.question: The government decides to auction import licenses under a quota system. Who captures the quota rents in this arrangement?
options: Foreign exporters, because they can charge higher prices for the restricted goods
The government, as competitive bidding transfers the quota rent to public revenue
Domestic consumers, through lower prices caused by the auction process itself
The rents disappear entirely because auctions eliminate all economic profits
questions.explanation: Competitive bidding drives the license price up to exactly the difference between the domestic and world price. The government collects the full quota rent as auction revenue.

questions.question: A small country imposes a 10% ad valorem tariff on smartphones with a world price of \$600. What is the new domestic price?
options: \$600, because small countries cannot raise domestic prices above the world level
\$660, calculated as the world price plus ten percent of that price as a tariff
\$610, reflecting only the fixed dollar amount of the tariff, not the percentage
\$540, because the tariff forces foreign suppliers to lower their pre-tax price
questions.explanation: Domestic price = World price + Tariff = \$600 + (\$600 \times 0.10) = \$600 + \$60 = \$660.

questions.question: On a standard partial equilibrium tariff diagram, which area represents the gain in domestic producer surplus from the tariff?
options: The rectangle representing the total government tariff revenue from imports
The area between the supply curve, the world price line, and the domestic price line
The triangle representing the deadweight loss from inefficient domestic production
The area below the demand curve and above the domestic price after the tariff
questions.explanation: Producers gain from receiving a higher price on existing output plus additional surplus from the expanded production made profitable by the tariff.

questions.question: A specific tariff of \$5 per unit is applied to a product with a world price of \$50. What is the equivalent ad valorem tariff rate?
options: 5%, calculated as the tariff divided by the domestic price after the tax
10%, equal to the specific tariff amount divided by the world price of the good
\$5, because specific tariffs are always expressed in absolute dollar terms only
50%, reflecting the ratio of the world price to the specific tariff per unit
questions.explanation: Ad valorem equivalent = Specific tariff ÷ World price = \$5 ÷ \$50 = 0.10, or 10% of the good's value.

questions.question: A country has a 20% tariff on finished bicycles but no tariff on imported bicycle parts. What is the Effective Rate of Protection for domestic bicycle assembly?
options: Exactly 20%, matching the nominal tariff rate on the finished product
Greater than 20%, because the tariff protects the full domestic value-added
Less than 20%, because some protection is lost through the duty-free inputs
Zero, because the absence of tariffs on parts cancels out the final good tariff
questions.explanation: With duty-free imported parts, the 20% nominal tariff protects the entire domestic value-added, magnifying the effective rate of protection above the nominal rate.

questions.question: Under a Voluntary Export Restraint, which party typically captures the economic rent created by the trade restriction?
options: The importing country's government through higher customs duties and taxes
The foreign exporting firms, because they administer the supply limit themselves
Domestic consumers in the importing country through lower final retail prices
The rent is always split equally between the importing and exporting governments
questions.explanation: Because the exporting country controls the restriction, its firms can charge the higher scarcity price in the importing market and retain the resulting quota rent.

questions.question: Which non-tariff barrier requires that a specified fraction of a product's value be sourced from domestic suppliers?
options: A voluntary export restraint that limits the foreign content of imported goods
A local content requirement that mandates the use of domestically produced inputs
A government procurement policy that favors domestic firms for public contracts
A technical barrier to trade based on domestic safety and testing standards
questions.explanation: Local content requirements force manufacturers to use domestic inputs even when cheaper foreign alternatives are available, protecting domestic component producers.

questions.question: Why might a binding import quota lead to higher domestic prices than an equivalent tariff in a market with growing demand?
options: Quotas are always set at more restrictive levels than equivalent tariff rates
The quota quantity is absolutely fixed, while tariff-restricted imports can grow with demand
Tariffs automatically adjust upward as demand increases, keeping prices higher than quotas
Quotas eliminate all foreign competition while tariffs still allow some imports to enter
questions.explanation: Under a tariff, additional imports can enter by paying the tax. Under a binding quota, import supply is perfectly inelastic at the cap, so demand growth drives prices much higher.

questions.question: What is administrative protection as a non-tariff barrier to international trade?
options: Government agencies that directly manage and operate all import-competing firms
Using slow customs procedures and complex regulations to discourage or delay imports
A formal system where government officials set individual import quotas for each firm
International administrative bodies that adjudicate trade disputes between nations
questions.explanation: Administrative protection raises the cost and time required to import through bureaucratic hurdles, paperwork, and regulatory complexity rather than explicit tariffs or quotas.

questions.question: A country runs a Current Account surplus. What does the identity $S - I = CA$ imply about its saving and investment?
options: National saving is exactly equal to domestic investment in the economy
National saving exceeds domestic investment, and the country is a net lender abroad
Domestic investment exceeds national saving, requiring foreign capital inflows
The Current Account and the saving-investment balance are completely unrelated
questions.explanation: From $S - I = CA$, a Current Account surplus means $S > I$. The excess of saving over investment is lent to the rest of the world.

questions.question: What types of international transactions are recorded in a country's Financial Account?
options: Exports and imports of goods, services, and unilateral transfer payments
Cross-border purchases and sales of financial assets, including FDI and portfolio flows
Interest and dividend income earned on foreign investments by domestic residents
Government foreign aid payments and remittances from workers living abroad
questions.explanation: The Financial Account records transactions in financial assets and liabilities: foreign direct investment, portfolio investment, bank flows, and reserve assets.

questions.question: A US citizen purchases shares of stock listed on the Tokyo Stock Exchange. How is this recorded in the US Balance of Payments?
options: As a credit in the Current Account reflecting the purchase of a foreign good
As a debit in the Financial Account because capital is flowing out of the United States
As a credit in the Financial Account reflecting increased foreign portfolio liabilities
As a debit in the Current Account under the services and investment income category
questions.explanation: Buying a foreign stock increases US ownership of foreign assets. This capital outflow is recorded as a debit in the US Financial Account.

questions.question: Why does the Statistical Discrepancy exist in every country's Balance of Payments?
options: It measures the difference between a country's nominal and real exchange rate values
Data for different accounts are collected from separate sources, creating measurement errors
It tracks unreported criminal and black-market international financial transactions
It records transactions that are too small to classify into any other BoP category
questions.explanation: Because trade, income, and financial data come from different and imperfect sources, the accounts rarely balance perfectly. The statistical discrepancy is the required plug figure.

questions.question: The nominal exchange rate is \$1.10 per Euro. A burger costs \$5.50 in the US and €4.00 in Germany. What is the real exchange rate (US goods per German good)?
options: 1.25, indicating German goods are cheaper than equivalent American goods
0.8, calculated as the nominal rate times the Euro price divided by the Dollar price
1.10, because the real rate always equals the nominal rate for consumer goods
4.40, reflecting the product of the exchange rate and the German price in Euros
questions.explanation: Real exchange rate = $(E \times P_{foreign}) / P_{domestic} = (1.10 \times 4.00) / 5.50 = 4.40 / 5.50 = 0.8$. The Euro is undervalued relative to PPP.

questions.question: What does the Uncovered Interest Parity condition predict about interest rate differentials between two countries?
options: Interest rates must always be identical across all countries to prevent any arbitrage
The interest rate differential should equal the expected percentage change in the exchange rate
Higher interest rates always cause immediate and permanent currency depreciation
Interest rate differentials and exchange rate expectations are completely unrelated
questions.explanation: UIP states that investors require a higher interest rate to hold a currency expected to depreciate. The differential equals the expected exchange rate change.

questions.question: What is a currency carry trade in international financial markets?
options: Physically transporting large quantities of cash across international borders by air
Borrowing funds in a low-interest currency to invest in a currency with higher interest rates
Trading currencies based strictly on their relative purchasing power parity valuations
A central bank strategy of diversifying its reserves across many different foreign currencies
questions.explanation: Carry traders profit from the interest rate spread between two currencies, assuming the exchange rate between them remains relatively stable over the investment period.

questions.question: What characterizes a managed float, also known as a dirty float, exchange rate system?
options: The exchange rate is permanently fixed by law and never changes under any circumstances
Market forces determine the rate, but the central bank intervenes to limit extreme fluctuations
A committee of commercial bank executives meets each week to set the official exchange rate
Multiple countries share a single currency managed by a supranational central banking authority
questions.explanation: A managed float combines market-determined exchange rates with occasional central bank intervention to smooth excessive short-term volatility or resist long-term trends.
